meta.title: Building a Kubernetes Operator in Go with Kubebuilder
meta.description: Walkthrough on creating a custom Kubernetes Operator using Kubebuilder: CRDs, controllers, finalizers, and leader election.
meta.ogTitle: Building a Kubernetes Operator in Go with Kubebuilder
meta.ogDescription: Walkthrough on creating a custom Kubernetes Operator using Kubebuilder: CRDs, controllers, finalizers, and leader election.

content:
Kubernetes Operators extend the Kubernetes API to automate the management of complex applications. Instead of relying on manual processes or external scripts, an operator encodes the operational knowledge of a service within the cluster itself. In this article we'll build an operator from scratch using Kubebuilder and controller-runtime, exploring CRDs, controllers, finalizers, and leader election.

What is an Operator?

An Operator is a custom controller that watches custom resources (CRDs) and executes actions to reconcile the current state with the desired state. The pattern was popularized by CoreOS and today is the de facto standard for extending Kubernetes.

Common use cases:

  • Database management (Prometheus, MySQL, PostgreSQL)
  • Automated updates with rollback (Etcd, Spark)
  • Backup and restore of stateful applications
  • Auto-scaling based on custom metrics

Scaffolding with Kubebuilder

Kubebuilder generates all the boilerplate: project structure, Dockerfile, YAML manifests, and Makefile configurations. You only need to define your API and reconciliation logic.

# Initialize the project
kubebuilder init domain --domain freyre.dev --repo github.com/freyre/my-operator

# Create the API (CRD + Controller)
kubebuilder create api --group cache --version v1alpha1 --kind Memcached --resource=true --controller=true

# Generate the manifests
make generate && make manifests

This creates the typical structure:

  • api/v1alpha1/memcached_types.go — CRD definition
  • internal/controller/memcached_controller.go — controller logic
  • config/crd/ — CRD manifests
  • config/samples/ — custom resource examples

Defining the CRD

The CRD is defined with Go structs using marker tags. Kubebuilder uses these markers to generate the OpenAPI v3 specification of the CRD.

// +kubebuilder:object:root=true
// +kubebuilder:subresource:status
// +kubebuilder:resource:path=memcacheds,scope=Namespaced
type Memcached struct {
  metav1.TypeMeta   `json:",inline"`
  metav1.ObjectMeta `json:"metadata,omitempty"`

  Spec   MemcachedSpec   `json:"spec,omitempty"`
  Status MemcachedStatus `json:"status,omitempty"`
}

type MemcachedSpec struct {
  // +kubebuilder:validation:Minimum=1
  // +kubebuilder:validation:Maximum=100
  Replicas int32 `json:"replicas,omitempty"`

  // +kubebuilder:validation:Pattern=`^\d+(\.\d+)?(M|G)?i?$`
  Memory string `json:"memory,omitempty"`
}

type MemcachedStatus struct {
  ReadyReplicas int32  `json:"readyReplicas,omitempty"`
  Conditions    []metav1.Condition `json:"conditions,omitempty"`
}

Reconciliation: the heart of the controller

The Reconcile method runs every time the state of the watched resource changes. Its responsibility is to read the current state and modify it until it matches the desired state.

func (r *MemcachedReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {
  log := log.FromContext(ctx)

  // 1. Get the resource
  var memcached cachev1alpha1.Memcached
  if err := r.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, &memcached); err != nil {
    return ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)
  }

  // 2. Define the desired Deployment
  dep := &appsv1.Deployment{
    ObjectMeta: metav1.ObjectMeta{
      Name:      req.Name,
      Namespace: req.Namespace,
    },
    Spec: appsv1.DeploymentSpec{
      Replicas: &memcached.Spec.Replicas,
      Selector: &metav1.LabelSelector{
        MatchLabels: map[string]string{"app": req.Name},
      },
      Template: corev1.PodTemplateSpec{
        ObjectMeta: metav1.ObjectMeta{
          Labels: map[string]string{"app": req.Name},
        },
        Spec: corev1.PodSpec{
          Containers: []corev1.Container{{
            Name:  "memcached",
            Image: "memcached:1.6",
            Args:  []string{"-m", memcached.Spec.Memory},
            Ports: []corev1.ContainerPort{{ContainerPort: 11211}},
          }},
        },
      },
    },
  }

  // 3. Apply (create or update)
  if err := controllerutil.SetControllerReference(&memcached, dep, r.Scheme); err != nil {
    return ctrl.Result{}, err
  }

  found := &appsv1.Deployment{}
  err := r.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, found)

  if err != nil {
    if client.IgnoreNotFound(err) != nil {
      return ctrl.Result{}, err
    }
    log.Info("Creating Deployment")
    if err := r.Create(ctx, dep); err != nil {
      return ctrl.Result{}, err
    }
  } else {
    // Update replicas if changed
    if *found.Spec.Replicas != memcached.Spec.Replicas {
      found.Spec.Replicas = &memcached.Spec.Replicas
      if err := r.Update(ctx, found); err != nil {
        return ctrl.Result{}, err
      }
    }
  }

  // 4. Update status
  memcached.Status.ReadyReplicas = found.Status.ReadyReplicas
  if err := r.Status().Update(ctx, &memcached); err != nil {
    return ctrl.Result{}, err
  }

  return ctrl.Result{}, nil
}

Finalizers: controlled cleanup

Finalizers prevent a resource from being deleted until the operator runs a cleanup. They are essential for resources that manage external infrastructure (PVs, load balancers, DNS).

const memcachedFinalizer = "cache.freyre.dev/finalizer"

func (r *MemcachedReconciler) reconcileFinalizers(ctx context.Context,
  memcached *cachev1alpha1.Memcached) (ctrl.Result, error) {

  if memcached.ObjectMeta.DeletionTimestamp.IsZero() {
    // The resource is not being deleted
    if !controllerutil.ContainsFinalizer(memcached, memcachedFinalizer) {
      controllerutil.AddFinalizer(memcached, memcachedFinalizer)
      if err := r.Update(ctx, memcached); err != nil {
        return ctrl.Result{}, err
      }
    }
  } else {
    // The resource is being deleted
    if controllerutil.ContainsFinalizer(memcached, memcachedFinalizer) {
      // Cleanup logic: delete external resources
      if err := r.cleanupExternalResources(ctx, memcached); err != nil {
        return ctrl.Result{}, err
      }
      controllerutil.RemoveFinalizer(memcached, memcachedFinalizer)
      if err := r.Update(ctx, memcached); err != nil {
        return ctrl.Result{}, err
      }
    }
  }
  return ctrl.Result{}, nil
}

Leader Election for high availability

When you run multiple replicas of the operator, only one should be active at a time. Kubebuilder integrates leader election using a ConfigMap or Lease. It is enabled with a flag:

func main() {
  var enableLeaderElection bool
  flag.BoolVar(&enableLeaderElection, "leader-elect", false,
    "Enable leader election for controller manager.")

  mgr, err := ctrl.NewManager(ctrl.GetConfigOrDie(), ctrl.Options{
    Scheme:                     scheme,
    LeaderElection:             enableLeaderElection,
    LeaderElectionID:           "my-operator.freyre.dev",
    LeaderElectionResourceLock: "leases",
  })
}

In production it is recommended to always enable it with at least 2 replicas. The default lease TTL is 15 seconds, configurable with LeaderElectionLeaseDuration.

Testing with envtest

Kubebuilder includes envtest, a framework that starts a local etcd and API server for integration tests without a real cluster.

var _ = Describe("Memcached controller", func() {
  ctx := context.Background()

  It("should create a Deployment from a Memcached CR", func() {
    By("Creating a Memcached resource")
    memcached := &cachev1alpha1.Memcached{
      ObjectMeta: metav1.ObjectMeta{
        Name:      "test-memcached",
        Namespace: "default",
      },
      Spec: cachev1alpha1.MemcachedSpec{
        Replicas: 3,
        Memory:   "128M",
      },
    }
    Expect(k8sClient.Create(ctx, memcached)).Should(Succeed())

    By("Checking the Deployment was created")
    dep := &appsv1.Deployment{}
    Eventually(func() error {
      return k8sClient.Get(ctx, types.NamespacedName{
        Name: "test-memcached", Namespace: "default",
      }, dep)
    }, time.Second*10, time.Millisecond*250).Should(Succeed())

    Expect(*dep.Spec.Replicas).Should(Equal(int32(3)))
  })
})

Deploying the operator

Kubebuilder generates a Makefile with targets for building and deploying. The typical flow is:

# Build and push the image
make docker-build docker-push IMG=registry.freyre.dev/my-operator:v1

# Install CRDs
make install

# Deploy the operator
make deploy IMG=registry.freyre.dev/my-operator:v1

# Create a sample resource
kubectl apply -f config/samples/cache_v1alpha1_memcached.yaml

Conclusion

Kubebuilder abstracts much of the complexity of building operators, allowing you to focus on business logic. In this article we covered CRDs, controllers, finalizers, and leader election — the pillars of any production-ready operator.

The operator we built is ready to be extended with advanced features: validation/mutation webhooks, Prometheus metrics, and chaos engineering tests. The full source code is available at github.com/FreyreCorona.
